content.text: A model with high {{c1::bias}} underfits the data, while high {{c2::variance}} overfits and fails to generalize.
content.explanation: The term "bias" refers to -variance tradeoff is a core concept for choosing models, regularization, and validation strategies.. In context: "A model with high bias underfits the data, while high variance overfits and fails to generalize." — memorizing this phrase helps you use it naturally in conversation.
content.spanish: Un modelo con alto sesgo subajusta los datos, mientras que alta varianza sobreajusta y falla en generalizar.
content.usage: Fundamental for supervised learning, model selection, and debugging poor performance.

content.text: Cross-validation estimates generalization by repeatedly splitting data into train and validation {{c1::folds}}.
content.explanation: It reduces the risk of reporting optimistic performance from a single lucky split.
content.spanish: La validación cruzada estima la generalización al dividir los datos en múltiples folds de entrenamiento y validación.
content.usage: Used in model comparison, hyperparameter tuning, and feature engineering workflows.

content.text: A {{c1::confounder}} can create a misleading association between a feature and the target if it is not controlled.
content.explanation: Confounding is central for causal reasoning and for avoiding false conclusions in experimentation.
content.spanish: Un factor de confusión puede crear una asociación engañosa entre una característica y la meta si no se controla.
content.usage: Important in observational studies, marketing analysis, and policy evaluation.

content.text: {{c1::Regularization}} adds a penalty to limit model complexity and reduce overfitting.
content.explanation: L1 and L2 regularization are standard tools for controlling variance in linear and neural models.
content.spanish: La regularización añade una penalización para limitar la complejidad del modelo y reducir el sobreajuste.
content.usage: Essential in logistic regression, SVMs, and deep learning optimization.

content.text: In imbalanced datasets, metrics like {{c1::precision}}, {{c2::recall}}, and {{c3::F1}} are often more informative than raw accuracy.
content.explanation: Class imbalance can make accuracy nearly useless when one class dominates the dataset.
content.spanish: En conjuntos con clases desbalanceadas, métricas como precisión, recall y F1 suelen ser más informativas que la exactitud pura.
content.usage: Common in fraud detection, rare disease prediction, and anomaly detection.

content.text: Feature engineering transforms raw {{c1::variables}} into representations that expose relevant structure to the model.
content.explanation: Good features often matter more than the algorithm itself in practical forecasting and tabular problems.
content.spanish: La ingeniería de características transforma variables crudas en representaciones que exponen estructura relevante al modelo.
content.usage: Central in credit scoring, churn prediction, and recommendation systems.

content.text: A calibrated model outputs probabilities that reflect {{c1::real}}-world confidence rather than just ranking examples.
content.explanation: Calibration is essential for risk estimation, medical diagnosis, and decision support.
content.spanish: Un modelo calibrado devuelve probabilidades que reflejan la confianza real en lugar de solo ordenar ejemplos.
content.usage: Used when probabilities matter more than hard labels.

content.text: {{c1::Distribution shift}} occurs when the data seen in production no longer matches the training distribution.
content.explanation: This is one of the most common causes of degraded real-world ML performance.
content.spanish: El cambio de distribución ocurre cuando los datos vistos en producción ya no coinciden con la distribución de entrenamiento.
content.usage: Critical for deployment monitoring, forecasting, and fraud systems.

content.scenario: Dual-Coding Diagram 📊 (Word2Vec CBOW vs. Skip-gram (Mikolov et al., 2013))
content.text: ¿Cómo funciona la estructura de {{c1::Word2Vec CBOW vs. Skip-gram (Mikolov et al., 2013)}} en el sistema?
content.explanation: CBOW predicts the current target word from surrounding context words, making it faster to train on frequent words. Skip-gram predicts the surrounding context words given the current target word, performing better with small datasets and representing rare words well.
content.spanish: CBOW predice la palabra objetivo actual basándose en las palabras de contexto vecinas, mientras que Skip-gram predice el contexto vecino dada la palabra objetivo actual.
content.usage:
Visual model for Word2Vec CBOW vs. Skip-gram (Mikolov et al., 2013).
Graph:
graph TD subgraph CBOW ["Continuous Bag of Words (CBOW)"] Context["Context words: x(t-2), x(t-1), x(t+1), x(t+2)"] --> Projection["Sum / Average projection layer"] Projection --> Target["Target word: y(t)"] end subgraph Skipgram ["Skip-gram"] Input["Input word: x(t)"] --> Projection_SG["Projection layer"] Projection_SG --> Output1["Target Context: y(t-2)"] Projection_SG --> Output2["Target Context: y(t-1)"] Projection_SG --> Output3["Target Context: y(t+1)"] Projection_SG --> Output4["Target Context: y(t+2)"] end
content.concept: Word2Vec CBOW vs. Skip-gram (Mikolov et al., 2013)

graph TD
    subgraph CBOW ["Continuous Bag of Words (CBOW)"]
        Context["Context words: x(t-2), x(t-1), x(t+1), x(t+2)"] --> Projection["Sum / Average projection layer"]
        Projection --> Target["Target word: y(t)"]
    end

        Projection_SG --> Output2["Target Context: y(t-1)"]
